TextENGNew: My mother asked me to go shopping, and on my way to the grocery store I ran into Satoko.
She told me she'd been asked to do the same kind of errand,
so we decided to go together.
We compared shopping lists and had fun guessing what the other family was having for dinner.
Satoko's family seemed to be having fish and simmered vegetables.
changeReason: 'grilled fish and cooked vegetables' changed to 'fish and simmered vegetables'; JPN 煮物 specifically means simmered/stewed dishes, not generically 'cooked'.

TextENGNew: Ours, apparently, was hamburger steak.
changeReason: 'Salisbury steak' changed to 'hamburger steak'; JPN ハンバーグ refers to Japanese hamburger steak, a different dish from Salisbury steak.

TextENGNew: ``My, hamburger steak—how envious!@k@|@y@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2002. The fish is fine, but the simmered vegetables worry me a bit.@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2003. I'm quite certain there's still plenty of pumpkin left from the other day.``
changeReason: Multiple changes: 'that sounds so very good' vs 'hamburger steak—how envious'; 'grilled fish' vs just 'fish'; 'vegetables I'm less fond of' vs 'simmered vegetables worry me a bit.' The correction from Salisbury to hamburger steak and from cooked to simmered better matches JPN.

TextENGNew: ``...Then tonight will surely be a pumpkin extravaganza.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2066. Poor, poor Satoko~☆``
changeReason: Old: 'I bet you'll be getting a lot tonight'; new: 'tonight will surely be a pumpkin extravaganza.' JPN カボチャの煮物三昧 means 'a feast/extravaganza of pumpkin simmered dishes'—more specific and vivid.

TextENGNew: ``It's rather depressing to know in advance that pumpkin is coming.@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2005. Haah...``
``...Mii~☆@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2068. What a spoiled Satoko—complaining no matter how the pumpkin arrives.``
changeReason: New adds 'What a spoiled Satoko' matching JPN 贅沢な沙都子 (extravagant/spoiled Satoko), which old omitted.

TextENGNew: ``R-Rika, surely even you have foods you dislike?!@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2007. That's right—don't you hate sweet things like daifuku and cream puffs?!``
changeReason: Old omits the category 'sweet things' (JPN 甘い物が嫌い); new correctly includes it, changing the meaning from disliking specific items to disliking the whole category.

TextENGNew: ``...I don't dislike them.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2070. I simply choose not to eat them, out of spite.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2071. If I felt like it, I could eat any number of them.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2072. I could even enjoy a nice pumpkin pie. Nipah~☆``
changeReason: New adds 'out of spite' matching JPN 意地悪で (out of stubbornness/spite), which old omitted entirely.

TextENGNew: ``Oooh, it's not fair that you aren't a picky eater at all!!``
Conversations like that were pure fun.
Satoko was my very best friend.
The grocery shopping I usually found so tedious was instantly transformed into a pleasant evening stroll, just by having Satoko at my side.
Near the supermarket, other shops like a prepared-foods vendor lined the street, forming a small shopping district.
This was the busiest time of day for dinner shopping.
Hinamizawa's housewives gathered here to do their shopping in a lively bustle.
Since Satoko and I needed different things, we split up to buy our items separately.
``Welcome, welcome!!@k@vS20/00/VTK2_souzai2002. Oh, Rika-chama!@k@vS20/00/VTK2_souzai2003. Doing the shopping?@k@vS20/00/VTK2_souzai2004. What a good girl!``
``...Nipah~☆@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2074. I'm a good girl who does the shopping all by herself.``
``That you are—so here's a little reward for being such a good girl!@k@vS20/00/VTK2_souzai2006. Here, take this.``
Hanging from the eaves was a sieve full of change for making transactions, along with several candies the shopkeeper kept on hand for his occasional cough.
changeReason: Old: 'candies for the owner's occasional asthma attack'; new: 'candies the shopkeeper kept on hand for his occasional cough.' JPN 咳き込む means coughing, not asthma—different medical condition.

TextENGNew: He plucked one out and tossed it to me.
It was strawberry milk flavored—my absolute favorite.
I tucked it into my pocket instead of eating it right away, so I could show off to Satoko later that I alone had received it.
For me, teasing Satoko and making her envious was one of life's great pleasures.
Satoko always responded with the most exaggerated reactions,
making her the finest companion imaginable—I was never bored in her company.
``Oh, Rika-chama, what's for dinner tonight?@k@vS20/00/VTK2_nikuya2002. Ground pork... hmm...``
``...Hamburger steak.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2076. We're going to put flower-shaped fried eggs on top and draw pictures with ketchup.``
changeReason: 'Salisbury steak' changed to 'Hamburger steak' matching JPN ハンバーグ.

TextENGNew: ``Oh ho ho ho! That sounds wonderful!``
Enjoying these pleasant exchanges, I gradually filled my shopping bag.
...Then, the whispered conversation of some housewives drifted into my ears.
It was... slightly cruel in content.
...They were talking about Satoko, who had come here to shop.
'How long do those traitors who tried to sell the village out to the government plan on staying here?'
changeReason: Old: 'How long do those village traitors plan to stay here?'; new adds 'who tried to sell the village out to the government' matching JPN 村を国に売る裏切り者.

TextENGNew: 'They should just leave already.'
'And they don't even have the nerve to come shopping themselves—they send their daughter instead.'
...On and on they went...
...Ever since the Houjou family and the Sonozaki main house clashed so explosively at the dam project's explanatory meeting,
changeReason: 'Sonozaki family' changed to 'Sonozaki main house' matching JPN 園崎本家.

TextENGNew: the Houjou family had sunk deeper and deeper into isolation within the village.
At first, there had been no small number of families who sided with the Houjous in favoring relocation.
But as the Sonozaki main house spearheaded a thorough campaign to align the village's stance, hardening it into a monolithic position, the pro-relocation voices gradually fell silent.
The overt harassment led by the Sonozaki and Kimiyoshi families—two of the Three Families with enormous influence in the village—had also played a significant role.
The Sonozaki family apparently used the Houjou family as a cautionary example to deter any would-be sympathizers from siding with the pro-relocation faction.
For villagers whose ancestors had lived here for generations, and who themselves expected to be buried in this same soil, such harassment exerted crushing pressure.
One had to wonder what became of those who had so boldly rallied behind the Houjous at the meeting, shouting fiery support as leaders of the pro-relocation faction...
changeReason: Old asks 'Who was it that agreed'; new asks 'what became of those who had so boldly rallied'—different question entirely. JPN asks what happened to those boisterous supporters.

TextENGNew: They had all gone quiet, and in a kind of loyalty test, they joined in the harassment of the Houjou family themselves...
changeReason: New adds 'in a kind of loyalty test' matching JPN 踏み絵的に (like a litmus test/loyalty test).

TextENGNew: The persecution of the Houjou family had now become pressure exerted by the entire village.
The Houjou family's reactions, inevitably shaped by the stifling atmosphere, became extreme in turn.
Before long, as if rising to the provocation, the family withdrew from the community council entirely.
I heard the trigger was something trivial... but it only worsened the Houjou family's standing further.
And after that upheaval, a campaign of insidious, village-wide harassment was heaped upon the Houjou family in every conceivable way.

The horror of what they endured spread through the village,@k intimidating people into believing they would suffer the same fate if they sympathized with the Houjous,@k ...driving the family ever deeper into isolation.
...The adults didn't discuss these behind-the-scenes machinations with the children, so at least Satoko was spared from torment at school.
...But it wasn't hard to imagine she lived every day in a suffocating tension that threatened to crush her...
Even now, the housewives who were muttering behind her back weren't saying any of it directly to Satoko.
...But that didn't mean she failed to notice the snippets that reached her ears, or the cold eyes trained upon her.
...When had this village become so cold toward Satoko?
...As her best friend, it broke my heart.
Looking at it this way, Satoko's shopping experience was nothing like my warm, friendly one.
When I greeted shopkeepers or made purchases, I received bonuses and special treatment. But Satoko got none of that.
No—it was worse. She was deliberately served last, deliberately ignored,@k and I could feel the air thick with people wanting nothing to do with her.
...Satoko had been weaving through the crowd of housewives, trying to tell the fishmonger what she wanted, but she was ignored to the very last.
...Satoko kept up a brave smile, pretending it didn't bother her... but I knew.
It was a painfully thin mask of endurance...
At that moment, Satoko dropped her coins all over the ground.
Perhaps her fingers slipped while she was trying to put the change back into her wallet.
I was some distance away, so I started weaving through the housewives to help her pick them up.
...And then I froze.
...Satoko had scattered coins everywhere, scrambling left and right to gather them—yet not a single person was helping her.
No, it wasn't just that no one helped. They were behaving as though Satoko simply wasn't there.
...Since she'd dropped them herself, Satoko wasn't exactly expecting anyone to help.
changeReason: Old says 'they're acting like they don't even see her' while JPN says 'since she dropped them herself, Satoko wasn't expecting anyone to help.' Different content.

TextENGNew: ...But even so... even so...
changeReason: Misaligned: JPN says '...But even so... even so...' but old contains previous entry's content about Satoko not expecting help.

TextENGNew: ...With all these people coming and going, not one of them was making the slightest effort to help...
``...Satoko, I'll help you pick them up.``
``Ah... ah... I-I'm fine, Rika...``
That small reaction alone told me everything about the wound in her heart.
And then—the moment I began picking up Satoko's coins, the other housewives started helping too.
As if it were the most natural thing in the world.
...So they'll help as long as a shrine maiden of the Furude family takes the initiative.
They would have pretended not to see until the very end if I hadn't stepped in...
And the fact that everyone only helped because 'I' did must have wounded Satoko even more deeply...
I snatched the coins from the housewives' hands without a word of thanks.
...Thanking them was the last thing on my mind. I wanted to spit at them.
``Th... Thank you so much.@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2011. Rika.@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2012. Everyone.``
...If she failed to express gratitude, it would only give people more ammunition to say the Houjou daughter had no manners.
Satoko forced the words of thanks past her lips, bitter as they tasted.
The housewives looking down at her wore blank, indifferent expressions... and I simply could not bear it.
...I could not bear the pain Satoko was carrying right now...
``...Once we're done shopping, let's go, Satoko.``
I grabbed Satoko's arm and pulled her away from that place.
...I was frustrated,@k heartbroken,@k and aching.
What sin has Satoko committed...?
changeReason: Old: 'What has Satoko done?'; new: 'What sin has Satoko committed...?' JPN 罪 means sin/crime—stronger word than 'done'.

TextENGNew: The cold cruelty of that reality was devastating.
I was treated like a mascot, adored by everyone.
changeReason: Old: 'Everyone loves me'; new: 'I was treated like a mascot, adored by everyone.' JPN マスコット (mascot) is omitted from old, changing the characterization.

TextENGNew: But I couldn't share any of that warmth with Satoko...
...Right now, Satoko must be wondering why she—a girl the same age as me—receives such utterly different treatment...
changeReason: New adds 'a girl the same age as me' from JPN 同い年, an important detail for understanding the unfairness.

TextENGNew: ...If being together only caused Satoko this kind of pain, perhaps I shouldn't have come shopping with her...@k No, that's wrong.
I should have done her shopping for her...
``...Satoko.``
``...Wh... What is it, Rika?``
``...Dropping all those coins was terrible luck.@k@vS20/05/VTK2_rika2081. ...So here—a consolation prize.``
changeReason: Old: 'something that'll make you happy'; new: 'a consolation prize.' JPN 残念賞 literally means consolation prize/booby prize—different connotation.

TextENGNew: I wanted to soothe Satoko's wounded heart, even if only slightly... so I pulled out the strawberry milk candy I had just received.
But... Satoko wouldn't take it.
``...That candy was given to you, wasn't it?``
My hand froze...
...Satoko had seen.
She had seen me receive this candy...
``...The person who gave it to you wanted you to eat it.@k@vS20/04/VTK2_satoko2017. ...So if I ate it, that would be rude...``
...Satoko turned her back to me, and walked that way in silence until we reached the fork where our paths diverged...
changeReason: New captures JPN とぼとぼ (trudging/plodding dejectedly) while old simply says 'continued on'.

TextENGNew: Without so much as a goodbye, Satoko trudged away with her shopping bags in hand.
...I had only wanted to ease Satoko's pain... yet at the very end, I had rubbed salt into her wound...
How dare I call myself her best friend?
If I had the strength in my grip, I would have crushed that candy in my fist right then and there...
